title: Complementarity between bosonic and fermionic many-body interferences with partially distinguishable particles
authors: Marco Robbio, Michael G. Jabbour, Nicolas J. Cerf
abstract: It is well known that bosons and fermions exhibit opposite behaviors when experiencing interference, in the sense that bosons have a tendency to bunch whereas fermions have a tendency to antibunch. Recently, this complementarity was mathematically characterized in [arXiv:2312.17709] by means of an identity relating the transition probabilities of both types of particles in a linear interferometer. Here, we show that such a complementarity still holds even when particles become partially distinguishable, for example, when they have slightly different polarizations or time delays. Namely, we establish a relation that combines bosonic and fermionic multiparticle interferences in an arbitrary linear interferometer, in the presence of partial distinguishability. Incidentally, this also provides a new mathematical identity relating the permanent and determinant of tensors of order 3. Importantly, this complementarity has direct operational consequences in quantum metrology. Indeed, we show that the correlation matrices for bosonic and fermionic particle number distributions at the output of the interferometer obey a simple sum rule: their sum equals twice the correlation matrix for classical particles. This, in turn, constraints the achievable quantum Fisher information in phase-estimation protocols, highlighting a trade-off whereby greater indistinguishability enhances bosonic sensitivity whereas reduced indistinguishability can benefit fermionic schemes.
summary: This paper establishes a formal mathematical complementarity between bosonic and fermionic interference in linear interferometers. It proves that even when particles are partially distinguishable, a fundamental sum rule holds between their correlation matrices and classical particles. The work reveals a metrological trade-off: increasing particle indistinguishability enhances bosonic phase sensitivity but can degrade fermionic sensitivity. Additionally, the findings provide new mathematical identities relating the permanent and determinant of tensors.
main_problem: The paper investigates the fundamental relationship and complementarity between bosonic and fermionic many-body interference when particles are partially distinguishable.
main_result: The authors establish an exact algebraic identity relating bosonic and fermionic transition probabilities and prove a sum rule where the sum of their covariance matrices equals twice that of classical particles.
method: The study employs multilinear algebra, generating functions, and the Khatri-Rao product, utilizing MacMahon's master theorem and Weingarten calculus for Haar random averages.
model_or_system: The system consists of bosons and fermions passing through an m-mode linear interferometer characterized by a unitary matrix U, where partial distinguishability is modeled via a Gram matrix S.
key_observables: Transition probabilities, particle number distributions, covariance matrices, and Quantum Fisher Information (QFI).
important_parameters: Degree of indistinguishability (Gram matrix S), unitary transformation U, and particle number (even vs. odd).
main_assumptions: The interferometer is assumed to be linear (unitary), and internal degrees of freedom are modeled as pure states; the analysis focuses on thermal and Fock input states.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a 50:50 beam-splitter illustrating bosonic bunching and fermionic antibunching; Figure 2 depicts a general protocol for phase estimation in a linear interferometer.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of bosonic-fermionic complementarity, develops a mathematical framework using generating functions and 3-tensors, proves fundamental theorems regarding transition probabilities and covariance matrices, and concludes with metrological implications and computational complexity considerations.
why_interesting: It provides a deep mathematical link between permanents and determinants in the context of quantum statistics, offering new insights into the limits of quantum metrology and the simulation of Boson/Fermion sampling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
